sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 49.7g of fat. saturated fat is a major pressure point. for seeds, the fat profile is one of the biggest reasons they earn their place.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 23.4g of carbs. starch is doing most of the heavy lifting here. for a seed, this is a very strong sign.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 17.7g of protein. essential amino acid support is one of the better parts here. protein is a bonus, but not enough by itself.

sections.narration: everything else here is pretty flat.

sections.title: Minerals
sections.narration: magnesium at 88% daily value, calcium at 98% daily value, iron at 81% daily value stand out most. that is major mineral support.
sections.subtitleText: magnesium at 88% daily value, calcium at 98% daily value, iron at 81% daily value stand out most. that is major mineral support.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: the upsides first: elite mineral density because the mineral payload is a major category strength beyond a normal seed baseline. easy to use across lots of foods because works well in savoury dishes, baking, and toppings. strong wholefood seed identity because feels like a very legitimate seed-category food rather than an edge case.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: now the downsides: contains some anti-nutrients because phytates may reduce mineral uptake somewhat. not suitable for everyone because sesame allergy risk matters in practice. saturated fat is not negligible because still not a disaster, but it is less clean than the best fat-profile seeds.

closing.summary: Sesame Seeds does have some solid upside. but the tradeoffs keep it from climbing higher. best as a support food that boosts meals instead of carrying them alone.
